findings.description: Body text and section typography are materially changed from the original serif memorandum style to a sans-serif web style, reducing visual distinction of the document's formal formatting.
findings.evidence: The PDF uses serif body text and more formal memorandum styling; the HTML uses sans-serif text throughout, with headings and body rendered in a different visual style. Content remains distinguishable, but formatting fidelity is reduced.

findings.description: Section headings and body formatting are not closely matched to the PDF's original typography.
findings.evidence: On page 2, the PDF shows bold serif section headers such as 'I. History/Background' and 'II. March 22, 2016 Public Meeting and Oral Comments Summary'; the HTML preserves hierarchy but renders them in a different sans-serif style and weight.

findings.description: List and paragraph styling differ from the original document's formal typesetting.
findings.evidence: The PDF uses serif text with underlined topic labels in bullets; the HTML keeps bullets and emphasis but changes the typographic presentation to a web-style sans-serif format.

findings.description: Written comment summary formatting is preserved semantically but not visually matched to the PDF typography.
findings.evidence: The PDF page uses bold serif heading 'III. Written Public Comment Summary' and serif bullet text; the HTML preserves structure and emphasis but with noticeably different font family and spacing.

findings.description: Bullet list typography and section styling differ from the PDF.
findings.evidence: The PDF uses serif bullets and underlined labels; the HTML presents the same list content in sans-serif with web-style spacing, preserving meaning but not original visual formatting.

findings.description: Attachment list formatting is preserved structurally but differs visually from the original memorandum style.
findings.evidence: The PDF shows 'IV. Attachments' in bold serif text with a numbered list below; the HTML keeps the section and numbering intact but renders them in a different sans-serif style.

findings.description: One oral-comment bullet lost list structure and was converted to a non-bulleted continuation item, altering the structure of the summary.
findings.evidence:
PDF page 3 shows a bulleted item labeled "Groundwater Assessments – Commenters referenced an expert report..." aligned with the other bullets. In the HTML, this appears as `
  • Groundwater Assessments ...
    • `, which suppresses the bullet and changes the visual/list structure.

findings.description: A phrase in the written comments summary contains OCR/conversion corruption that changes the sentence text.
findings.evidence: PDF page 4, under "Costs," reads: "A majority of the comments were requests that Duke not pass on their cost to the consumers." HTML reads: "A majority of the comments were requests de that Duke not pass on their cost to the consumers." The extra "de" is not in the PDF.

findings.description: A sentence in the Surface Water summary is missing words, changing grammar and slightly degrading meaning.
findings.evidence: PDF page 4 reads: "Other comments were regarding NCDEQ cited Duke Energy for broken, failing corrugated metal storm water pipes at Buck that are cracked and leaking..." HTML reads: "Other comments were regarding NCDEQ cited Duke Energy for broken, failing corrugated metal storm water pipes at Buck that are cracked and leaking..." Compared to the PDF image, the intended phrasing is "Other comments were regarding NCDEQ citing/citations to Duke Energy..."; the HTML appears to preserve a malformed phrase from OCR rather than a clean transcription.

findings.description: The top letterhead metadata and memorandum fields are not represented as semantic headings, so the heading structure captures only the memo title and numbered sections.
findings.evidence: HTML uses one `<h1>` for "MEMORANDUM" and `<h2>` for sections I-IV. The document's header names (PAT MCCRORY, DONALD R. VAN DER VAART, S. JAY ZIMMERMAN) and memo metadata (To/From/Subject) are rendered as paragraphs/table cells rather than headings. This is acceptable but not a fully expressive hierarchy.
